title: Expanding the Timeline: Continuous & Future Tenses
learningGoal: Master the present continuous tense ('ing') using 'raha' and the future tense using 'ga/ge/gi'.
instructions: In this block, we finally break out of the present tense. You will learn how to say what you are doing right now, and what you will do tomorrow. Pay extremely close attention to how gender and numbers affect verb endings.
conceptTitle: The 'Raha' Helper and the Future Suffixes

lessonContent.sectionTitle: 1. Present Continuous (I am eating / He is going)
lessonContent.bodyText:
To say you are doing something *right now*, Hindi uses a helper word: 'Raha' (रहा).

Formula: [Subject] + [Object] + [Verb Root] + [Raha/Rahe/Rahi] + [Hoon/Hai/Hain/Ho].

The helper word 'Raha' changes based on the gender and number of the SUBJECT:
- Singular Masculine: रहा (Raha)
- Plural/Formal Masculine: रहे (Rahe)
- Feminine (Singular & Plural): रही (Rahi)

Example 1 (Masculine I): Main kha raha hoon. (मैं खा रहा हूँ।) - I am eating.
Example 2 (Feminine I): Main kha rahi hoon. (मैं खा रही हूँ।) - I am eating.
Example 3 (Plural/Formal): Aap ja rahe hain. (आप जा रहे हैं।) - You are going.

Let's apply 'Raha' to some common verbs.
- To do = Karna -> Root = Kar. 'What are you doing?' (Formal) -> Aap kya kar rahe hain? (आप क्या कर रहे हैं?)
- To read = Padhna -> Root = Padh. 'He is reading a book.' -> Woh kitaab padh raha hai. (वह किताब पढ़ रहा है।)
- To come = Aana -> Root = Aa. 'They are coming.' -> Ve aa rahe hain. (वे आ रहे हैं।)

lessonContent.sectionTitle: 3. The Future Tense (Will go / Will eat)
lessonContent.bodyText:
The future tense in Hindi is formed by attaching suffixes directly to the verb root. Just like 'Raha', the future suffix changes based on gender and number: Ga (गा), Ge (गे), and Gi (गी).

Let's use the verb 'Jaana' (to go) -> Root 'Jaa'.

1. Main (I) -> Jaa + unga (masc) / ungi (fem)
- Main jaunga (मैं जाऊँगा) / Main jaungi (मैं जाऊँगी)

2. Tum (You informal) -> Jaa + oge (masc) / ogi (fem)
- Tum jaoge (तुम जाओगे) / Tum jaogi (तुम जाओगी)

3. Aap (You formal) / Hum (We) / Ye,Ve (They) -> Jaa + enge (masc) / engi (fem)
- Aap jayenge (आप जाएँगे) / Hum jayenge (हम जाएँगे)

4. Yeh/Woh (He/She) -> Jaa + ega (masc) / egi (fem)
- Woh jayega (वह जाएगा) / Woh jayegi (वह जाएगी)

targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: I will go to America tomorrow. (Female speaker)

lessonContent.bodyText:
A massive quirk of Hindi: The word 'Kal' (कल) means BOTH Yesterday and Tomorrow. 

How do you know which one it is? You look at the verb tense!
- Kal main ghar jaunga. (Tomorrow I will go home.) -> Future tense.
- Kal main ghar mein tha. (Yesterday I was in the house.) -> Past tense.

Let's learn 'Today' and 'Now' as well:
- Aaj (आज) : Today
- Abhi (अभी) : Right now

In Month 1, we learned 'Kijiye' (Please do). If you want to make a request even softer and more polite (like 'Would you please do this later?'), you add the future marker 'ga' to the formal command.

- Formal Command: Pijiye (Please drink)
- Soft Future Request: Pijiye ga (Please drink it whenever you are ready)
- Formal Command: Aaiye (Please come)
- Soft Future Request: Aaiye ga (Please come later / Do come over)

targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: Please come to our house tomorrow.

learningObjective: The learner will master the present continuous ('raha') and the future tense suffixes ('ga/ge/gi'), accurately matching them to the gender and number of the subject.

examples.explanation: Aap (formal) takes the plural masculine helper 'rahe', followed by the plural verb 'hain'.
Main takes the 'ungi' future suffix for a female speaker.

importantRules.ruleText: In continuous and future tenses, the verb MUST match the gender of the SUBJECT doing the action.
importantRules.exceptionNotes: Unlike Month 1 where adjectives matched the object (e.g., Mahangi gaadi), here the verb matches the person. A man driving a car says 'Main chala RAHA hoon', not 'rahi'.

importantRules.ruleText: The feminine continuous marker is ALWAYS 'rahi', regardless of whether it is singular or plural. 'Woh aa rahi hai' (She is coming) and 'Ve aa rahi hain' (They [women] are coming).
importantRules.exceptionNotes: Only the final verb (hai vs hain) changes to show plural for women.

cultureContext.contextText: The phrase 'Aa raha hoon' (I am coming) is universally used in India to mean 'I will be there eventually, maybe in 5 minutes, maybe in an hour.' If someone calls and asks where you are, you say 'Bas aa raha hoon' (Just coming).
dosList: Use 'Abhi' (Right now) if you want to emphasize that something is happening immediately.
dontsList: Do not assume 'Kal' means tomorrow without checking the verb tense.

summary: You have dramatically expanded your timeline. You can now describe actions happening right now using Raha/Rahe/Rahi, and actions that will happen using the Ga/Ge/Gi suffixes. You also learned that the verb must always match the gender of the speaker.

checkpointQuestions.questionText: If a female is speaking, how does she say 'I am going'?
checkpointQuestions.expectedAnswer: Main ja rahi hoon. (मैं जा रही हूँ।)
Yesterday OR Tomorrow, depending on the tense.

checkpointQuestions.questionText: Which future suffix is used for 'Aap' (You formal masculine)?

keyTakeaways: Add 'ga' to formal commands for ultra-polite requests.
adminCorrectAnswerSet.conceptSummary: Mastery of timeline shifting: Present Continuous and Simple Future.
importantFacts: Verbs in these tenses match the gender of the subject, not the object.
adminCorrectAnswerSet.expectedUnderstanding: Learners can express ongoing actions and make future plans.

feedbackRules.onPass: Astounding! You have mastered the Hindi timeline.
feedbackRules.onFail: Review Section 1 and 3. The gender of the speaker controls the verb endings ('a' for men, 'i' for women).
feedbackRules.onPartial: Good effort. Pay close attention to the plural/formal 'Aap'. It always takes the 'e' sound (Rahe, enge).

audioAssets.audioTranscript: मैं जा रहा हूँ। वह पानी पी रही है। आप क्या कर रहे हैं? मैं कल काम करूँगा। वह अभी आ रही है।

hintText: If you are a woman, get used to ending almost all your verbs in 'i' (rahi hoon, jaungi).
commonMistakes: A female speaker saying 'Main kha raha hoon'.
